query: Alopecia Areata vs other types of hair loss

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia -- Epidemiology -- Nonscarring Alopecia Frequency
retrieved_docs.content: Androgenetic alopecia: 37.7% Alopecia areata: 18.2% Telogen effluvium: 11.3%

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Totalis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Alopecia totalis (AT) is a chronic condition marked by complete scalp hair loss, which affects a small percentage of patients with alopecia areata (AA). AA is a chronic, autoimmune disorder of nonscarring hair loss mediated by T-lymphocytes against the hair follicular unit. The most common form of disease manifests as smooth, discrete, round patches of hair loss and can be referred to as alopecia areata focalis. AT is a more extensive subtype of AA characterized by complete loss of hair on the scalp. When there is a total loss of scalp and body hair, it is called alopecia universalis.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Totalis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Some differentials for AT include: Telogen effluvium Anagen effluvium Androgenetic alopecia

retrieved_docs.title: Androgenetic Alopecia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnoses of androgenetic alopecia encompass other hair loss disorders such as: Alopecia areata Anagen effluvium Syphilis Systemic diseases Telogen effluvium

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Totalis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Alopecia areata (AA) is a chronic, autoimmune disorder of nonscarring hair loss mediated by T-lymphocytes against the hair follicular unit (see Image . Alopecia Areata, Localized Hair Loss). [1] The most common form of disease manifests as smooth, discrete, round patches of hair loss and can be referred to as alopecia areata focalis (AF). Alopecia totalis (AT) is a more extensive subtype of AA characterized by complete loss of hair on the scalp. [2] When there is a total loss of scalp and body hair, it is called alopecia universalis (AU). Compared to focal AA, patients with AT have a poorer prognosis for hair regrowth and are more likely to have treatment failure (see Image. Alopecia Totalis With Regrowth). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Treatment / Management -- Immunotherapy and JAK Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with extensive disease, often characterized by more than 50% hair loss on the scalp, may explore treatment alternatives such as topical immunotherapy or oral JAK inhibitors to reduce the necessity of numerous injections associated with intralesional corticosteroids. Moreover, a retrospective study revealed the superior efficacy of topical immunotherapy compared to intralesional corticosteroids in patients with hair loss patches exceeding 50 cm². [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical manifestations and characteristic features of alopecia areata for accurate diagnosis. Screen patients for signs and symptoms indicative of alopecia areata during routine examinations. Implement evidence-based treatment strategies based on the severity and extent of hair loss in patients with alopecia areata. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare providers to coordinate care and provide holistic support to patients with alopecia areata by addressing both physical and psychosocial needs. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Various subtypes of alopecia are confused with each other and require detailed examination through dermoscopy and biopsy. Some of the differentials include: Androgenetic alopecia: Alopecia areata, telogen effluvium, traction alopecia, trichotillomania, FFA, LPP, and central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia [1] Alopecia areata: Tinea capitis, trichotillomania, and temporal triangular alopecia for patchy alopecia areata and female-pattern hair loss, telogen effluvium, and drug-induced alopecia for diffuse forms of alopecia areata [6] Telogen effluvium: Androgenetic alopecia and diffuse forms of alopecia areata [44] FFA and LPP: Chronic cutaneous lupus erythematosus, central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia, and folliculitis decalvans [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia -- History and Physical -- Alopecia areata
retrieved_docs.content: The Severity of Alopecia Tool or SALT score can help guide therapeutic decision-making and monitor the therapeutic response in patients with alopecia areata. [24] [25] The scalp area is divided into 4 quadrants, with each quadrant representing a percentage of the total scalp area: right side (18%), left side (18%), top (40%), and back (24%). The percentage of hair loss is visually estimated in each quadrant and then summated to determine the SALT score, with the 5 subgroups of hair involvement as follows: S0: No hair loss S1: Less than 25% hair loss S2: 25% to 49% hair loss S3: 50% to 74% hair loss S4: 75% to 99% hair loss S5: 100% hair loss [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: A 2019 multicenter retrospective study was performed to analyze "the frequency of the types of alopecia in patients consulting at specialist hair clinics and to assess for global variations." [12] Over 3000 diagnoses of alopecia were included, of which 73% were nonscarring, and 27% were scarring.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The frequency of the other alopecia types listed is less than 2%. The article also provides "differences in the frequency of hair disorders" for gender, median age range by gender, and continent. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia -- Evaluation -- Telogen effluvium
retrieved_docs.content: Telogen effluvium is typically diagnosed clinically. If needed, additional investigations may include a complete blood count, iron studies (serum ferritin, serum iron, and total iron binding capacity), TSH, T3, and T4 because 2 common conditions associated with telogen effluvium are iron deficiency anemia and thyroid disorders. [34] A serum zinc level and antinuclear antibody titer may also be indicated. [35] Scalp biopsy for acute telogen effluvium (shedding lasting for less than 6 months) will show an increase in telogen follicles. Normal counts are 6% to 13%; more than 15% suggests telogen effluvium, and more than 25% is definitive. A scalp biopsy can also help to rule out female pattern hair loss and alopecia areata. [34] Multiple biopsies increase the diagnostic accuracy of chronic telogen effluvium, with an anagen-to-telogen ratio of 8:1 compared with 14:1 with normal scalp biopsies. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A skin biopsy from the periphery of an active hair loss patch may be beneficial if the diagnosis remains uncertain. [1] Although alopecia areata is associated with other autoimmune diseases, current evidence does not advocate for routine screening unless the patient's clinical history suggests their presence. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The hair cycle consists of 3 phases—anagen, catagen, and telogen. During the anagen phase, active hair growth occurs, while the catagen phase involves the keratinization of the proximal end of the hair shaft. Subsequently, the hair follicle sheds and the remaining proximal end undergoes apoptosis. Telogen signifies the interval between the regression of the old follicle and the onset of the next anagen phase.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Areata -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Alopecia areata is a chronic, immune-mediated autoimmune disorder that affects hair follicles, nails, and, occasionally, the retinal pigment epithelium. This condition targets the anagen hair follicles of individuals and leads to hair loss without permanent damage to the follicles. The classic presentation of alopecia areata involves isolated, smooth, sudden, nonscarring, and patchy hair loss on the scalp or any area with hair growth. This condition arises from an autoimmune disruption in the normal hair cycle, resulting in the loss of immune privilege in the hair follicles.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichotillomania -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Other forms of hair loss must be placed on the differential diagnosis. Examples include traction alopecia, male pattern baldness, pressure alopecia, alopecia areata, tinea capitis, short-term habit, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and systemic diseases like cancer, lupus, hypothyroidism, and factitious disorder. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1965
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid dermatological preparations, plain encompass a range of topical corticosteroid strengths that are classified by potency groups. Within this category, “Corticosteroids, weak (group I)” represent the low‑potency end of the spectrum, while “Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV)” represent the high‑potency end. This hierarchical classification allows clinicians to select an appropriate plain corticosteroid preparation based on the severity of the skin condition, aiming to achieve therapeutic effectiveness while minimizing the risk of adverse effects associated with higher‑potency steroids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
retrieved_docs.content: are classified as plain dermatological preparations, representing distinct potency subclasses of simple topical steroids. Conversely, “Corticosteroids, weak, other combinations”, “Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations”, and “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations” are subclasses of “corticosteroids, other combinations”, indicating formulation with additional agents rather than as stand‑alone preparations. The hierarchical organization of potency groups (weak, moderately potent, potent, very potent) and formulation types (plain vs. combination) clarifies how hydrokortison and related corticosteroids are positioned across therapeutic protocols, dermatologic products, and combination drug regimens, underscoring their central role in the management of inflammatory skin conditions and related disorders.

Plain dermatological corticosteroid preparations constitute a subset of dermatological products and can be formulated across the full potency spectrum—from weak (group I) through moderately potent (group II) to very potent (group IV)—allowing therapy to be tailored to skin disease severity. The taxonomy further defines “corticosteroids, weak/moderately potent/potent, other combinations” as subtypes of the general “corticosteroids, other combinations” class. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate is noted as a therapeutic option for patients in a vegetative state, reflecting its role in critical care.
